The Christological catalog: the servants' fate is the fate of every prophet sent to call Israel to account. The pattern of rejection is not new — it is the recurring tragedy of the vineyard.
21.36: A revelation of God: "Again he sent other servants, more than the first. And they did the same to them." The repeated sending — more servants, the same rejection — is the divine patience with Israel across the centuries. The Christological bearing: the master does not give up after the first rejection. The multiplication of servants (prophets) before sending the son is the measure of the master's longsuffering. The final servant is not the first response but the culmination of a long history of attempts.
21.37: A direct revelation: "Finally he sent his son to them, saying, 'They will respect my son.'" The sending of the son after all the servants have been rejected is the Christological identification of the parable's central point: Jesus is the Son sent last, after all the prophets. The master's expectation — "they will respect my son" — is the divine logic of incarnation: the Son, as the final and definitive word (Heb 1:1-2), will be recognized as the one the servants could not be. The expectation is tragically wrong — not the Father's miscalculation but the measure of the tenants' wickedness.
21.38: A direct revelation: "But when the tenants saw the son, they said to themselves, 'This is the heir. Come, let us kill him and have his inheritance.'" The recognition of the son — "this is the heir" — and the deliberate decision to kill him is the Christological indictment of the chief priests and Sanhedrin: they know who he is (or suspect it), and they choose murder over submission. The plan to seize the inheritance by killing the heir is the logic of those who want the kingdom without the king.
21.39: A direct revelation: "And they took him and threw him out of the vineyard and killed him." The killing of the son — thrown out of the vineyard before being killed — has the Christological detail that mirrors the passion: Jesus is led outside the city to be crucified (27:32; cf. Heb 13:12: "Jesus suffered outside the gate"). The parable predicts the spatial pattern of the passion: expelled from the city, then killed. The allegory is precise in its prophecy.
21.40: A direct revelation: "When therefore the owner of the vineyard comes, what will he do to those tenants?" The question Jesus poses to the chief priests will produce their own judgment on themselves. The Christological irony: the chief priests are about to pronounce their own eschatological sentence.
21.41: A direct revelation: "They said to him, 'He will put those wretches to a miserable death and let out the vineyard to other tenants who will give him the fruits in their seasons.'" The chief priests' own verdict — the wretches destroyed, the vineyard given to new tenants — is the Christological self-judgment. Without knowing it, they have announced the eschatological consequence of rejecting the Son: the vineyard will be taken and given to others (v.43). The judgment they pronounce is accurate and will be applied to them.
21.43: A direct revelation: "Therefore I tell you, the kingdom of God will be taken away from you and given to a people producing its fruits." The direct application of the parable to the chief priests is the Christological verdict: the vineyard (the kingdom of God) will be given to a people who produce fruit — the community of Jew and Gentile gathered around the crucified and risen Son. The Christological transfer: the nation-as-exclusive-vessel of the kingdom is superseded by the community constituted by faith in the Son.
21.44: A direct revelation: "And the one who falls on this stone will be broken to pieces; and when it falls on anyone, it will crush him." The stone of stumbling — Jesus as the cornerstone — causes two different forms of judgment: those who fall on it are broken; those on whom it falls are crushed. The Christological severity: rejection of the cornerstone is not merely religious failure but eschatological catastrophe. The same stone that is the foundation of the new temple (v.42) is the stone of judgment for those who refuse it (Isa 8:14-15; Dan 2:34-35, 44).
21.45: A revelation of God: "When the chief priests and the Pharisees heard his parables, they perceived that he was speaking about them." The recognition of self-incrimination — "he was speaking about them" — is the Christological moment of dawning clarity. The parables have been transparent enough that those with ears have heard. The chief priests and Pharisees now know exactly what Jesus thinks of their position, and they will respond not with repentance but with plotting.
21.46: A revelation of God: "And although they were seeking to arrest him, they feared the crowds, because they held him to be a prophet." The same political calculation as in 21:26 (fearing the crowd about John) now applies to Jesus. The Christological protection: the popular recognition of Jesus as a prophet prevents the arrest the Sanhedrin wants. The crowd's limited identification (prophet, not Messiah) is still sufficient to protect him — temporarily. The chief priests will find their opening in Judas (26:14-16), who avoids the crowds by enabling a nighttime arrest.

22.44: A fulfillment: the LORD said to my Lord, sit at my right hand until I make your enemies a footstool for your feet. Jesus's use of Ps 110:1 reveals that the Messiah's identity exceeds Davidic sonship — he is David's Lord, which means he is the divine Son who pre-exists his own Davidic lineage. The Son of Man coming on the clouds (24:30) is seated at God's right hand (Ps 110:1) while his enemies are being subdued — the enthronement is present and active, not merely future. Every exercise of Christ's authority in Matthew (healing, teaching, forgiving, commissioning) is the exercise of this right-hand authority from the beginning of his ministry.
22.1: A revelation of God: "And again Jesus spoke to them in parables, saying..." The continued parabolic address to the chief priests and Pharisees links the Wedding Banquet parable to the Wicked Tenants. The Christological frame: Jesus keeps teaching the religious authorities through parables — giving them opportunity to hear, while the form itself reveals their inability to receive.
22.2: A direct revelation: "The kingdom of heaven may be compared to a king who gave a wedding feast for his son." The Wedding Banquet parable is the most explicit allegory in the parables: the king is the Father, the son is the Son (Jesus), and the wedding feast is the eschatological celebration of the kingdom. The Christological identification: the entire history of salvation is the Father's preparation of the wedding feast to honor his Son. Rejecting the invitation is rejecting the Son whom the feast honors.
22.3: A revelation of God: "And sent his servants to call those who were invited to the wedding feast, but they were not willing to come." The first sending — to those already invited (Israel, particularly the religious leadership) — is refused. "Not willing to come" (ouk ēthelon elthein) is the volitional refusal: not inability but unwillingness. The Christological bearing: the kingdom's invitation to those with the most preparation and expectation is the first and most culpable refusal.
22.4: A revelation of God: "Again he sent other servants, saying, 'Tell those who are invited, 'See, I have prepared my dinner, my oxen and my fat calves have been slaughtered, and everything is ready. Come to the wedding feast.''" The repeated, urgent invitation — "everything is ready" — is the urgency of the gospel: the preparation is complete, the feast is set, only the guests' presence is lacking. The Christological urgency: the Son has come, the sacrifice has been made (the oxen and fat calves anticipate the cross), and the invitation is extended again.
22.5: A revelation of God: "But they paid no attention and went off, one to his farm, another to his business." The indifference to the royal invitation — turning to ordinary business rather than the wedding — is the Christological diagnosis of the thorn-soil: "cares of the world and deceitfulness of riches choke the word" (13:22). The farm and business are not evil; the Christological problem is their priority over the kingdom's invitation. Ordinary life becomes the refusal of eschatological grace.
22.6: A revelation of God: "While the rest seized his servants, treated them shamefully, and killed them." The violence against the servants mirrors the Wicked Tenants parable: the progression from indifference (v.5) to active hostility. The Christological pattern: rejection of the kingdom invitation escalates from inattention to persecution. The servants killed are the prophets and the apostles who bore the invitation.
22.7: A revelation of God: "The king was angry, and he sent his troops and destroyed those murderers and burned their city." The burning of the city by the king's troops is a transparent reference to Jerusalem's destruction in 70 AD — the eschatological consequence of rejecting the Son's invitation. The Christological judgment: the murder of the servants and the rejection of the Son's wedding feast bring the judgment that Jesus predicted throughout the discourse (23:37-38; 24:1-2).
22.8: A revelation of God: "Then he said to his servants, 'The wedding feast is ready, but those who were invited were not worthy.'" The unworthiness of the first-invited guests is not a fixed characteristic but the verdict on their refusal. The Christological inversion: those who seemed most worthy (the religious leaders, those long-prepared) proved unworthy by their refusal; the unworthy (Gentiles, sinners, outsiders) will be made worthy by accepting the invitation.
22.9: A revelation of God: "Go therefore to the main roads and invite to the wedding feast as many as you find." The going to the main roads — the broad roads where all kinds of people travel — is the Christological Great Commission in parabolic form. The invitation goes to everyone regardless of preparation or qualification. The "therefore" connects the new mission to the rejection of the first: because the first-invited refused, the whole world becomes the field of invitation.
22.10: A revelation of God: "And those servants went out into the roads and gathered all whom they found, both bad and good. So the wedding hall was filled with guests." The mixed assembly — bad and good — is the Christological reality of the present kingdom: both wheat and weeds (13:24-30), both good fish and bad (13:47-50). The full wedding hall represents the church's present mixed condition. The final separation (vv.11-13) is the eschatological judgment that distinguishes genuine from false response.
22.11: A revelation of God: "But when the king came in to look at the guests, he saw there a man who had no wedding garment." The man without a wedding garment represents those who enter the community of the called without the garment of genuine righteousness — the "putting on Christ" (Rom 13:14) that constitutes genuine response. The Christological bearing: entry into the feast-community is necessary but not sufficient; the garment of new life is required. The wedding garment is not earned but given — refusing it is the second form of refusal.
22.12: A revelation of God: "And he said to him, 'Friend, how did you get in here without a wedding garment?' And he was speechless." The "Friend" (hetaire) is the same address as the workers (20:13) and Judas (26:50) — the address to those who are in proximity but not in genuine relationship. The speechlessness before the king is the Christological image of the judgment day when no defense is possible (cf. Rom 3:19: "every mouth may be stopped"). The one without the garment has nothing to say.
22.13: A revelation of God: "Then the king said to the attendants, 'Bind him hand and foot and cast him into the outer darkness. In that place there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth.'" The outer darkness and weeping — the seventh occurrence of this formula in Matthew (8:12; 13:42, 50; 22:13; 24:51; 25:30) — is the Christological refrain of eschatological exclusion. Those who refuse the invitation and those who enter without the garment share the same end: exclusion from the feast. The kingdom's joy and the judgment's darkness are the two destinations.
22.14: A direct revelation: "For many are called, but few are chosen." The closing maxim of the Wedding Banquet parable is the Christological summary of election and response: the call goes to all ("many"), but the chosen are those who receive it genuinely. The Christological tension: the invitation is universal (go to all the roads), but not all who hear it receive it with the genuine response that the garment represents. Election is not hostility to the un-chosen but the fulfillment of the grace genuinely received.
22.15: A revelation of God: "Then the Pharisees went and plotted how to entangle him in his talk." The conspiracy to trap Jesus in his speech is the Christological confrontation with hostile testing. The three tests that follow (Caesar, resurrection, greatest commandment) are political, theological, and legal traps designed to make Jesus choose a position that alienates some constituency. The one who speaks only truth answers each trap without being caught.
22.16: A revelation of God: "And they sent their disciples to him, along with the Herodians, saying, 'Teacher, we know that you are true and teach the way of God truthfully, and you do not care about anyone's opinion, for you are not swayed by appearances.'" The flattery before the trap is the Christological test of vanity: will Jesus be moved by praise? The affirmations they make — true, teaches God's way, not swayed by appearances — are all accurate descriptions of the Son of God. The trap is set inside a genuine description of who he is.
22.17: A revelation of God: "Tell us, then, what you think. Is it lawful to pay taxes to Caesar, or not?" The Caesar-question is a political dilemma: say yes, alienate the nationalists; say no, invite Roman charges of sedition. The Christological response will transcend the binary by reframing the question at a higher level — not which human authority to obey but what belongs to whom.
22.18: A revelation of God: "But Jesus, aware of their malice, said, 'Why put me to the test, you hypocrites?'" The awareness of their malice — divine perception of hidden motive — is the Christological knowledge of the human heart (cf. 9:4; 12:25). The naming of the test as malice is the prophetic unmasking: behind the flattery is hostility. The "hypocrites" charge names the gap between the flattering words and the malicious intent.
22.19: A revelation of God: "Show me the coin for the tax.' And they brought him a denarius." The coin produced belongs to the tribute money — a Roman denarius bearing the emperor's image and inscription. The Christological economy: Jesus does not refuse the coin or its use; he uses it to draw the distinction between imperial and divine claims.
22.20: A revelation of God: "And Jesus said to them, 'Whose likeness and inscription is this?' They said, 'Caesar's.'" The image and inscription on the coin are the tokens of Caesar's ownership-claim. The Christological counterpart: human beings bear the image of God (eikōn tou theou, Gen 1:26-27) — the inscription of God's ownership. The coin with Caesar's image belongs to Caesar; the person with God's image belongs to God.
22.21: A direct revelation: "He said to them, 'Therefore render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's, and to God the things that are God's.' When they heard it, they marveled and left him and went away." The "render to God what is God's" is the Christological trump card: everything ultimately belongs to God, including Caesar himself. The astonishment of the questioners is the recognition that they have been out-argued at the deepest level — the question about taxes has become a question about the image of God in every human being. The trap failed.
22.22: A revelation of God: "When they heard it, they marveled and left him and went away." The departure of the Pharisees and Herodians — without a further word — is the Christological victory in the controversy. They came with a trap; they leave with the trap unanswered in their hands. The one who answers unanswerable questions has authority they cannot match.
22.23: A direct revelation: "The same day Sadducees came to him, who say that there is no resurrection, and they asked him a question." The Sadducees — who accepted only the Pentateuch and denied resurrection — present the resurrection-dilemma. The Christological context: Jesus is himself the resurrection (John 11:25) and is about to demonstrate it. The one being questioned about resurrection will be raised from the dead within the week. The question is asked in the presence of its answer.
22.24: A revelation of God: "Saying, 'Teacher, Moses said, If a man dies having no children, his brother must marry the widow and raise up offspring for his brother.'" The levirate marriage law (Deut 25:5-6) is the Sadducean setup for the absurd scenario of v.25-28. The Christological bearing: they use Mosaic law to argue against resurrection, but Jesus will use Mosaic law to argue for it (v.31-32: Exod 3:6). The same Torah contains both the levirate law and the resurrection-implication; Jesus reads it with more depth than those who reject the resurrection.
22.25: A revelation of God: "Now there were seven brothers among us. The first married and died, and having no offspring left his wife to his brother." The hypothetical chain of seven levirate marriages — designed to make resurrection seem absurd — is the Sadducean thought experiment. The Christological response will not engage the hypothetical directly but will expose the faulty premise: resurrection life is not the continuation of earthly categories.
22.26: A revelation of God: "So too the second and third, down to the seventh." The escalation to seven (the number of completeness) makes the hypothetical maximally absurd. The Christological response will show that the absurdity is created by assuming resurrection = resuscitation. The new creation body is not constrained by the institutions of the old creation.
22.27: A revelation of God: "After them all, the woman died." The woman's death after all seven husbands completes the hypothetical. The Sadducean question assumes the woman's resurrection entails a legal conflict: which husband? The Christological answer will dissolve the conflict by dissolving the premise.
22.28: A revelation of God: "In the resurrection, therefore, of the seven, whose wife will she be? For they all had her." The "therefore" of the Sadducean question is their logical conclusion: resurrection creates an insoluble legal problem. The Christological reply will expose that the "therefore" rests on a misunderstanding of what resurrection is.
22.29: A direct revelation: "But Jesus answered them, 'You are wrong, because you know neither the Scriptures nor the power of God.'" The double charge — wrong about Scripture and wrong about divine power — is the Christological authority of the one who knows both. The Sadducees' error is not merely hermeneutical but theological: they have limited God's power to what they know from earthly experience. The one who will rise from the dead tells them their limits.
22.30: A direct revelation: "For in the resurrection they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like angels in heaven." The resurrection state is not the extension of earthly institutions but a new mode of existence. The Christological bearing: the one who will be the first to be raised (1 Cor 15:20) announces the shape of resurrection life. Marriage belongs to the old creation order (Gen 2:18-25); the new creation involves a different mode of communal existence. The Sadducean hypothetical dissolves because its premise (resurrection = earthly life continued) is false.
22.31: A direct revelation: "And as for the resurrection of the dead, have you not read what was said to you by God..." The "said to you by God" (to rhēthen hymin hypo tou theou) is the Christological reading of Scripture: these words in Exodus are not merely historical record but divine address to every hearer in every generation. The same God who spoke to Moses speaks through the text to those who read it. The living Scripture addresses the living community.
22.32: A direct revelation: "'I am the God of Abraham, and the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob'? He is not God of the dead, but of the living." The Christological argument from Exod 3:6 is logical and theological: God says "I am" (present tense) the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — who had been dead for centuries when Moses heard this. If God is their God in the present tense, they must be alive. God is in a living relationship with them; therefore they live. The one who makes this argument will himself be alive after death to confirm it.
22.33: A revelation of God: "And when the crowd heard it, they were astonished at his teaching." The crowd's astonishment at the resurrection-argument is the Christological effect of teaching with authority. The Sadducees' challenge has been turned into an argument for resurrection from within the Pentateuch they accepted. The one who teaches like this teaches not as a scribe but as the one who gave the Scriptures and knows their deepest intent.
22.34: A revelation of God: "But when the Pharisees heard that he had silenced the Sadducees, they gathered together." The Pharisees' regrouping after the Sadducees' failure is the Christological relay of opposition: each group takes a turn testing Jesus and failing. The gathering of the Pharisees after the Sadducees' defeat is the sustained opposition that will end only at the cross.
22.35: A revelation of God: "And one of them, a lawyer, asked him a question to test him." The lawyer's question — "to test him" (peirazōn, the temptation-word) — is the third Christological test of the Jerusalem week, following the Caesar-question and the resurrection-question. The testing continues; the one being tested is the same one who passed the wilderness test and every subsequent test.
22.36: A revelation of God: "Teacher, which is the great commandment in the Law?" The greatest-commandment question was a live rabbinic debate. The Christological significance: Jesus will give an answer that is not merely the best of the competing options but the interpretive key to the entire Torah. His answer does not choose between candidates but identifies the structure that holds all commandments together.
22.37: A direct revelation: "And he said to him, 'You shall love the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your mind.'" The citation of Deut 6:5 (the Shema's love-command) as the first and great commandment is the Christological affirmation of Israel's central confession. The one who cites "love the Lord your God" is himself the object of that love — the one in whom "the Lord your God" has become present. Loving God with all one's heart, soul, and mind is, in Matthew's Christology, loving the Son through whom the Father is made known.
22.38: A direct revelation: "This is the great and first commandment." The identification of the love-commandment as "great and first" (megalē kai prōtē) establishes it as the foundation from which all other commandments are derived and interpreted. The Christological bearing: the entire moral teaching of Jesus throughout the Gospel (the Sermon on the Mount, the community discourse, the conflict stories) is the explication of this first commandment — love for God that takes form in concrete human relationships.
22.39: A direct revelation: "And a second is like it: You shall love your neighbor as yourself." The citation of Lev 19:18 as the second commandment "like" the first establishes the inseparability of love for God and love for neighbor. The Christological integration: the one who embodies both — who loves the Father perfectly and gives himself for his neighbors completely — is the one who cites both as the summary of Torah. The two-commandment summary is the description of Jesus' own life.
22.40: A direct revelation: "On these two commandments depend all the Law and the Prophets." The hanging of "all the Law and the Prophets" on the two love-commandments is the Christological hermeneutic that has governed Jesus' teaching throughout Matthew. The Law and the Prophets (5:17: "I have come not to abolish them but to fulfill them") are fulfilled by the one who loves God perfectly and loves his neighbor to death. The cross is the supreme act of the double love-commandment.
22.41: A direct revelation: "Now while the Pharisees were gathered together, Jesus asked them a question." The Christological reversal: after three tests in which the Pharisees tested Jesus, Jesus now asks the definitive question. The questioner becomes the questioned. The one who has answered every test now poses the test that cannot be answered from within their framework.
22.42: A direct revelation: "Saying, 'What do you think about the Christ? Whose son is he?' They said to him, 'The son of David.'" The Pharisees' answer — "Son of David" — is correct as far as it goes (the Davidic descent of the Messiah is the standard expectation). The Christological trap: "Son of David" is true but insufficient. The Messiah is more than David's descendant; he is David's Lord. The question will expose the inadequacy of their messianic categories.
22.43: A direct revelation: "He said to them, 'How is it then that David, in the Spirit, calls him Lord, saying...'" The "in the Spirit" (en pneumati) identifies the source of Ps 110's authority: David did not write from political imagination but under divine inspiration. The Christological hermeneutic: Scripture is Spirit-inspired, which means its meaning exceeds what the human author consciously intended. David in the Spirit wrote about the Messiah he could not fully see — and Jesus, who is the Messiah, can read what David's Spirit-given words point toward.
22.45: A direct revelation: "If then David calls him Lord, how is he his son?" The logical question — if David (the ancestor) calls the Messiah "Lord" (the superior), how can the Messiah be merely David's descendant? — is the Christological demonstration of the inadequacy of the "Son of David" category alone. The Messiah must be more than human descent can explain. He must be, as the Nicene Creed will say, "God from God" — the divine Son who entered David's line, not merely the human product of that line.
22.46: A direct revelation: "And no one was able to answer him a word, nor from that day did anyone dare to ask him any more questions." The silencing of all questioners — Pharisees, Herodians, Sadducees, lawyers — is the Christological authority-demonstration of the Jerusalem controversy. He has answered every test perfectly, exposed every trap, and posed one question that no one can answer. The silence before the Son of God is the prelude to the trial where they will finally succeed — not by answering him but by bribing Judas and manipulating Pilate.

26.28: A fulfillment: this is my blood of the covenant which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins. The cup-word at the Last Supper is the most concentrated intersection of Christological and covenantal themes in Matthew: the blood is covenant-blood (Exod 24:8), it is poured out for many (Isa 53:12), it accomplishes forgiveness (Jer 31:34), it is Jesus's own blood (divine-human person). The Passover context adds the liberation-from-Egypt dimension: as the lamb's blood protected Israel on the night of exodus, so Christ's blood accomplishes the definitive exodus from sin and death. The institution of the Lord's Supper is the establishment of the new Passover memorial.
26.1: A direct revelation: "When Jesus had finished all these sayings, he said to his disciples..." The fifth and final closing formula of the Matthean discourses ("when Jesus had finished...") marks the completion of the Olivet Discourse (chs. 24-25) and Jesus' entire public teaching ministry. The Christological pivot: what follows is not teaching but action — the passion narrative that his entire ministry has been anticipating. The teacher who taught becomes the sacrifice he taught about.
26.2: A direct revelation: "You know that after two days the Passover is coming, and the Son of Man will be delivered up to be crucified." The self-prediction of crucifixion tied to the Passover timing is the Christological foreknowledge of the passion's details and schedule. The Son of Man who will be "delivered up" (paradidotai) is the Passover lamb who will be sacrificed at the Passover. The Christological identification of Jesus with the Passover lamb is implicit here and will become explicit in Paul (1 Cor 5:7: "Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed").
26.3: A revelation of God: "Then the chief priests and the elders of the people gathered in the palace of the high priest, whose name was Caiaphas." The gathering of the Sanhedrin leadership at Caiaphas's palace is the human-political side of the passion's machinery. The Christological irony: while Jesus is predicting his crucifixion to the disciples, the authorities are meeting to plan it. The two-track narrative — divine foreknowledge and human plotting — will converge at the cross.
26.4: A revelation of God: "And plotted together in order to arrest Jesus by stealth and kill him." The plot to arrest Jesus by stealth — avoiding the public during the festival — is the political calculation that has been building since 21:46. The Christological bearing: the authorities must work in secret because the public recognizes something in Jesus that the authorities refuse to acknowledge. The crowd's protection of Jesus (his popularity) forces the conspiracy underground.
26.5: A revelation of God: "But they said, 'Not during the feast, lest there be an uproar among the people.'" The decision to avoid the feast (Passover week) is overridden by Judas's offer (v.14-16) — the betrayal provides the covert access that the authorities need. The Christological irony: the very timing they sought to avoid (Passover) becomes the time chosen by the Father for the sacrifice of the true Passover lamb. Human calculation is overtaken by divine economy.
26.6: A direct revelation: "Now when Jesus was at Bethany in the house of Simon the leper..." The Bethany setting — two miles from Jerusalem, the home of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus (John 12:1-8 parallels this scene) — is the place of the last recorded act of devotion before the passion. The Christological reception: Jesus receives in Bethany what he will not receive in Jerusalem. The anointing that prepares for death comes from those who love him, not those who will kill him.
26.7: A direct revelation: "A woman came up to him with an alabaster flask of very expensive ointment, and she poured it on his head as he reclined at table." The anointing of the head is the royal anointing — the act by which kings were consecrated in Israel (1 Sam 10:1; 16:13: Samuel anointed Saul and David on the head). The woman who anoints Jesus on the eve of his death is enacting a coronation that the authorities are simultaneously plotting to prevent. The Christ — the Anointed One — receives his anointing.
26.8: A revelation of God: "And when the disciples saw it, they were indignant, saying, 'Why this waste?'" The disciples' indignation at the extravagance is the economic calculation that misses the Christological significance. The "waste" they see is, from the perspective of the woman's love and Jesus' coming death, the most fitting use of the ointment. The disciples have learned about service and sacrifice but have not yet understood that some expressions of devotion to Christ exceed economic calculation.
26.9: A revelation of God: "For this could have been sold for a large sum and given to the poor." The reasonable alternative — sell and give to the poor — is not wrong in itself (cf. 25:35-36: the poor must be served). The Christological priority: the woman has understood what the disciples have not — that the one present before them is the one through whom all other service is oriented. The anointing of Christ is not an alternative to serving the poor; it is the act that defines the meaning of all service.
26.10: A direct revelation: "But Jesus, aware of this, said to them, 'Why do you trouble the woman? For she has done a beautiful thing to me.'" The "beautiful thing" (kalon ergon, literally "good/beautiful work") is the Christological affirmation of extravagant devotion. The awareness of the disciples' reaction (before they speak) is the Christological divine perception. The one who commends her act as beautiful is the one who knows its full significance — the anointing of the body before burial.
26.11: A direct revelation: "For you always have the poor with you, but you will not always have me." The saying about the poor is not a dismissal of the poor but a Christological claim about his own uniqueness and temporal presence. The poor will always require service (Deut 15:11: "the poor will always be with you in the land") — that obligation is permanent. But the opportunity to anoint the embodied Son of God before his death is singular. The Christological present tense: he will not always be with them in this form.
26.12: A direct revelation: "In pouring this ointment on my body, she has done it to prepare me for burial." The Christological interpretation of the anointing as burial preparation is Jesus' own reading of the woman's act — perhaps beyond her own conscious intention. He receives a royal anointing and reads it as the preparation for the burial that is coming. The one who will be anointed for kingship (the triumphal entry) is about to be anointed for burial. Both are true; the cross is the throne.
26.13: A direct revelation: "Truly, I say to you, wherever this gospel is proclaimed in the whole world, what she has done will also be told in memory of her." The promise that the woman's act will be told "wherever this gospel is proclaimed in the whole world" is the Christological claim about his own death's universal significance. The gospel proclaimed throughout the world (24:14) includes the story of this woman who understood something the disciples did not. Her act of preparation for his death is inseparable from the gospel of his death — the good news is that he died, and she prepared him for it.
26.14: A shadow: "Then one of the twelve, who was called Judas Iscariot, went to the chief priests." The immediate juxtaposition of Judas's departure with the woman's extravagant devotion is the Christological contrast of the passion narrative's opening: one disciple anoints Jesus for burial in love; another disciple delivers him for execution for money. The "one of the twelve" — the insider status named — is the Christological wound of betrayal by the near, the familiar, the one who ate at the same table.
26.15: A direct revelation: "And said, 'What will you give me if I deliver him over to you?' And they paid him thirty pieces of silver." The thirty pieces of silver is the fulfillment of Zech 11:12-13 (the shepherd valued at the price of a slave, Exod 21:32, and the money thrown to the potter). The Christological irony: the one who is worth more than many sparrows (10:31), who is the cornerstone rejected by the builders (21:42), is sold for the price of a slave. The transaction that the authorities make exposes their valuation of the Son of God.
26.16: A shadow: "And from that moment he sought an opportunity to betray him." The seeking of an opportunity — the conspiracy now activated within the inner circle — is the Christological tragedy of Judas. He has been present for all of Jesus' teaching and ministry; he was among the twelve sent out with authority (10:1-4). The betrayal is not the act of an outsider but of one who had been given everything and chose to sell it for thirty pieces of silver.
26.17: A direct revelation: "Now on the first day of Unleavened Bread the disciples came to Jesus, saying, 'Where will you have us prepare for you to eat the Passover?'" The disciples' question about the Passover preparation is the Christological setting of the Last Supper: the Passover meal, the annual memorial of the Exodus from Egypt, will become the occasion for the institution of the new covenant meal that memorializes the new Exodus from sin. The typological structure: Passover lamb (Exodus) is replaced by Passover Lamb (Christ).
26.18: A direct revelation: "He said, 'Go into the city to a certain man and say to him, 'The Teacher says, My time is at hand. I will keep the Passover at your house with my disciples.''" The "my time is at hand" (ho kairos mou engys estin) is the Christological announcement of the passion's timing: the appointed hour that Jesus has been pointing toward throughout the Gospel has arrived. The same divine timing that governs the mission (cf. John 7:8: "my time has not yet fully come") now arrives. The master of time announces the hour.
26.19: A revelation of God: "And the disciples did as Jesus had directed them, and they prepared the Passover." The preparation of the Passover by the disciples at Jesus' direction is the Christological execution of the divine plan. The disciples who prepare the Passover are preparing the setting for the institution of the new covenant meal — though they do not yet know it. The Passover they prepare will be the last Passover before the reality it pointed to is accomplished.
26.20: A revelation of God: "When it was evening, he reclined at table with the twelve." The evening Passover meal reclining with the twelve is the Christological gathering of the community before the passion. The "twelve" named — Judas is included — is the Christological precision: the community that gathers around the table includes the betrayer. The table fellowship that Jesus has practiced throughout his ministry (eating with tax collectors, Pharisees, crowds) reaches its climax at this final table.
26.21: A direct revelation: "And as they were eating, he said, 'Truly, I say to you, one of you will betray me.'" The announcement of the betrayal at the table is the Christological moment of prophetic self-disclosure: Jesus knows who will betray him and names the betrayal before it happens. The "one of you" — addressed to all twelve — is the Christological individual accountability: each must examine himself. The table that offers communion also requires self-examination.
26.22: A revelation of God: "And they were very sorrowful and began to say to him one after another, 'Is it I, Lord?'" The sorrow of all twelve and the question "is it I, Lord?" is the Christological invitation to self-examination. Each disciple knows his own potential for failure; none confidently excludes himself. The "Lord" (Kyrie) of the question is the recognition that the one who knows the betrayer is the one before whom each is examined. The table is the place of self-disclosure.
26.23: A direct revelation: "He answered, 'He who has dipped his hand in the dish with me will betray me.'" The identification of the betrayer as the table-companion is the Christological fulfillment of Ps 41:9: "Even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me." The Psalm quoted by John 13:18 is cited by the pattern here. The intimacy of dipping in the same bowl — the shared meal — makes the betrayal the ultimate violation of covenant friendship.
26.24: A direct revelation: "The Son of Man goes as it is written of him, but woe to that man by whom the Son of Man is betrayed! It would have been better for that man if he had not been born." The "goes as it is written" is the Christological double movement: the death is divinely appointed (Isa 53; Dan 9:26) and the betrayer is personally culpable. The divine necessity does not remove human responsibility. The woe on the betrayer — "better if he had not been born" — is the Christological seriousness about the weight of this act. Both the sovereignty of God and the freedom of Judas are held together.
26.25: A direct revelation: "Judas, who would betray him, answered, 'Is it I, Rabbi?' He said to him, 'You have said so.'" The Christological confirmation — "you have said so" (sy eipas) — is the same formula Jesus will use with Pilate (27:11) and the high priest (26:64). It is neither a denial nor a full confession but the turning of the question back on the asker. Judas has identified himself by his own words. The "Rabbi" (teacher) rather than "Lord" (Kyrie) of the other disciples is the Christological distance in Judas's address: he does not call him Lord.
26.26: A direct revelation: "Now as they were eating, Jesus took bread, and after blessing it broke it and gave it to the disciples, and said, 'Take, eat; this is my body.'" The institution of the Eucharist — took, blessed, broke, gave — is the fourfold eucharistic action repeated from the feeding miracles (14:19; 15:36). The "this is my body" (touto estin to sōma mou) is the Christological identification of the bread with his physical person. The act of giving the bread is the act of giving himself — the self-offering that the cross will complete is here enacted in anticipation.
26.27: A direct revelation: "And he took a cup, and when he had given thanks he gave it to them, saying, 'Drink of it, all of you..'" The command "drink of it, all of you" is the Christological universality of the cup's offering — not some but all. The cup that represents his blood is available to every disciple at the table. The Christological grace: the one who will pour out his blood for many opens the cup to all who will receive it. The exclusive becomes inclusive at the table the Son sets.
26.29: A direct revelation: "I tell you I will not drink again of this fruit of the vine until that day when I drink it new with you in my Father's kingdom." The eschatological projection — "until that day when I drink it new with you in my Father's kingdom" — is the Christological promise that the Last Supper is not the final supper but the penultimate one. The new drinking in the Father's kingdom is the consummation of what the eucharist anticipates: the full presence of the risen Son with his people in the new creation. The Passover that began in Egypt will end in the kingdom.
26.30: A revelation of God: "And when they had sung a hymn, they went out to the Mount of Olives." The Passover hymns (the Hallel, Ps 113-118) sung at the conclusion of the meal carry the Christological weight of the Psalms' praise: the one who sings "I will not die but live, and tell of the works of the LORD" (Ps 118:17) is about to die. The Mount of Olives — where the Transfiguration prefigured glory (17:1-8) and the Olivet Discourse anticipated the parousia — is the setting for Gethsemane, where the passion begins.
26.31: A direct revelation: "Then Jesus said to them, 'You will all fall away because of me this night. For it is written, 'I will strike the shepherd, and the sheep of the flock will be scattered.''" The citation of Zech 13:7 ("strike the shepherd, and the sheep will be scattered") is the Christological self-identification as the shepherd who will be struck. The shepherd imagery is YHWH's own (Ezek 34: "I myself will be the shepherd of my sheep"), and Jesus, who has called himself the good shepherd (John 10:11), now identifies with the shepherd struck by the divine sword. The scattering of the disciples will fulfill the prophecy.
26.32: A direct revelation: "But after I am raised up, I will go before you to Galilee." The resurrection prediction attached to the passion prediction is the Christological pattern of every passion announcement in Matthew: death and resurrection are inseparable. "I will go before you to Galilee" is the promised re-gathering after the scattering (v.31) — the shepherd struck will be raised and will re-gather the scattered sheep. The resurrection is not a postscript but the resolution of the passion.
26.33: A revelation of God: "Peter answered him, 'Though they all fall away because of you, I will never fall away.'" Peter's confident self-exception — "I will never fall away" (ego oudepote skandalisthēsomai) — is the Christological irony of one who does not yet know himself. The Christological bearing: the one who confessed "you are the Christ, the Son of the living God" (16:16) will deny knowing the Christ three times within hours. The gap between Peter's self-confidence and his actual condition is the human condition that grace must fill.
26.34: A direct revelation: "Jesus said to him, 'Truly, I say to you, this very night, before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times.'" The precise prediction — this very night, before the rooster crows, three times — is the Christological foreknowledge of Peter's specific failure. The specificity is not cruel but compassionate: Jesus names the denial before it happens to prepare Peter for what comes after it. The one who predicts the denial also says "I will go before you" (v.32) — the denier will be received back.
26.35: A revelation of God: "Peter said to him, 'Even if I must die with you, I will not deny you!' And all the disciples said the same." All twelve make the promise; all twelve will break it (v.56: "all the disciples left him and fled"). The Christological instruction in human frailty: the disciples who make the most fervent promises of fidelity are those who have not yet been tested by the Gethsemane darkness. The gap between the table's vow and the arrest's flight is the gap that only resurrection can bridge.
26.36: A direct revelation: "Then Jesus went with them to a place called Gethsemane, and he said to his disciples, 'Sit here, while I go over there and pray.'" The Gethsemane prayer — the deepest exposure of the Son's relationship with the Father before the passion — is introduced by the command to "sit here while I pray." The Christological separation: even the disciples cannot enter the innermost space of the Son's prayer. The prayer is the Son's private dialogue with the Father in the hour of the cross's approach.
26.37: A direct revelation: "And taking with him Peter and the two sons of Zebedee, he began to be sorrowful and troubled." The taking of Peter, James, and John — the Transfiguration inner circle — into the Gethsemane vigil is the Christological inverse of the Transfiguration: on the mountain, they saw his glory; in the garden, they witness his agony. "Began to be sorrowful and troubled" (ērxato lupeisthai kai adēmonein) is the full human suffering of the incarnate Son — genuine emotional anguish before the approaching cup.
26.38: A direct revelation: "Then he said to them, 'My soul is very sorrowful, even to death; remain here, and watch with me.'" The "my soul is very sorrowful, even to death" (perilypos estin hē psychē mou heōs thanatou) echoes the language of Ps 42:5, 11 and 43:5 (the psalms of the soul cast down, crying out to God). The Christological depth: the Son of God at Gethsemane is the embodiment of the Psalms' anguish — the genuine human experience of facing death in fellowship with the Father. The request to "watch with me" is the Son's need for human companionship in his darkest hour.
26.39: A direct revelation: "And going a little farther he fell on his face and prayed, saying, 'My Father, if it be possible, let this cup pass from me; nevertheless, not as I will, but as you will.'" The Gethsemane prayer is the Christological summit of obedience: the genuine human will ("let this cup pass from me") submitted to the divine will ("as you will"). The cup of divine wrath (Ps 75:8; Isa 51:17; Jer 25:15-29) that the Father asks the Son to drink is not evaded but embraced. The "not as I will" is the Son's voluntary surrender to the cross — the free act that makes the atonement genuinely voluntary.
26.40: A revelation of God: "And he came to the disciples and found them sleeping. And he said to Peter, 'So, could you not watch with me one hour?'" The sleeping disciples — "could you not watch with me one hour?" — is the Christological loneliness of Gethsemane: the inner circle cannot sustain even an hour of wakefulness with him. The one who will drink the cup of wrath alone finds his companions asleep. The Christological bearing: the passion is ultimately borne alone, because no human companion could enter the space where the Son faces the Father's judgment in the sinners' place.
26.41: A direct revelation: "Watch and pray that you may not enter into temptation. The spirit indeed is willing, but the flesh is weak." The watchfulness-and-prayer command is the Christological teaching embedded in the rebuke: the disciples' sleep is not malice but weakness. "The spirit is willing, but the flesh is weak" is the Christological diagnosis of human limitation — the same disciples who vowed to die with him cannot stay awake. The prayer that sustains the willing spirit against the weak flesh is the prayer the Son himself is modeling.
26.42: A direct revelation: "Again, for the second time, he went away and prayed, 'My Father, if this cannot pass unless I drink it, your will be done.'" The second prayer — "if this cannot pass unless I drink it, your will be done" — is the Christological deepening of submission: the first prayer asks if there is another way; the second accepts that there is not. The "your will be done" is the Lord's Prayer's petition (6:10) prayed by the one who instituted it, in the moment when it costs everything. The Son who taught "your will be done" now demonstrates what it costs to pray it.
26.43: A revelation of God: "And again he came and found them sleeping, for their eyes were heavy." The disciples' continued sleep — eyes heavy — is the Christological solidarity of human weakness: the body's demand overrides the spirit's intention. The one who prays in the darkness returns twice to find them sleeping. The isolation of Gethsemane is complete: the Father's cup must be drunk alone, because no human companion can remain awake through the night of the passion's approach.
26.44: A revelation of God: "So, leaving them again, he went away and prayed for the third time, saying the same words again." The third prayer — "the same words" — is the Christological persistence in the face of the unanswered-from-the-human-perspective prayer. The cup will not pass; the Father's will stands. The three prayers mirror the three denials Peter will make within hours — the Son prays three times in submission; the disciple denies three times in fear. The contrast is the measure of the difference between the Son and the failing disciple.
26.45: A direct revelation: "Then he came to the disciples and said to them, 'Sleep and take your rest later on. See, the hour is at hand, and the Son of Man is betrayed into the hands of sinners.'" The "the hour is at hand" (hē hōra ēngiken) is the Christological announcement of the passion's beginning. The "hour" that Jesus has been pointing toward throughout the Gospel (cf. John 2:4; 7:30; 8:20; 12:23, 27) has arrived. The Son of Man who predicted his betrayal (26:2, 24) now announces its imminent fulfillment. The prayer is complete; the arrest is coming.
26.46: A direct revelation: "Rise, let us be going; see, my betrayer is at hand." The "rise, let us be going" is the Christological voluntary movement toward the arrest. Jesus does not flee, hide, or wait to be found; he moves toward the approaching betrayer. The passive "delivered up" of the passion predictions becomes the active "let us go" of the actual moment. The Son submits to the Father's will not by staying still but by going forward to meet it.
26.47: A revelation of God: "While he was still speaking, Judas came, one of the twelve, and with him a great crowd with swords and clubs, from the chief priests and the elders of the people." The crowd with swords and clubs — a show of force against one man at prayer — is the Christological contrast of power and vulnerability. The armed crowd sent by the chief priests and elders comes for the Son of God, who has just prayed "your will be done." The force of the arrest is the ironic excess of those who fear the one they are arresting.
26.48: A shadow: "Now the betrayer had given them a sign, saying, 'The one I will kiss is the man; seize him.'" The kiss of betrayal is the Christological perversion of the greeting: the kiss of friendship becomes the sign of identification for arrest. In the ancient world, the kiss was the sign of intimate relationship (cf. Prov 27:6: "faithful are the wounds of a friend, but deceitful are the kisses of an enemy"). The betrayal uses the form of love to accomplish its opposite.
26.49: A shadow: "And he came up to Jesus at once and said, 'Greetings, Rabbi!' And he kissed him." The "Greetings, Rabbi!" with the kiss is the Christological double-faced moment: the public greeting, the private signal. The "Rabbi" — teacher, not Lord — is Judas's last address to Jesus. The one who has been with Jesus throughout the ministry greets him with a word of honor and the gesture of intimacy while handing him over to the armed crowd.
26.50: A direct revelation: "Jesus said to him, 'Friend, do what you came to do.'" The "Friend" (hetaire) — the same address used to the uncommitted in 20:13 and 22:12 — is the Christological last word of Jesus to Judas: not condemnation but the name for those who are in proximity but not in genuine relationship. "Do what you came to do" is the Christological sovereignty over the betrayal: Jesus does not resist, does not call it off, does not expose Judas to the crowd. He permits what the Father has appointed.
26.51: A revelation of God: "And behold, one of those who were with Jesus stretched out his hand and drew his sword and struck the servant of the high priest and cut off his ear." Peter's sword-drawing (identified as Peter in John 18:10) is the misguided defense that Jesus immediately counters. The Christological rebuke that follows (v.52) shows that violent resistance is not the way of the kingdom, even when the one being arrested is the Son of God.
26.52: A direct revelation: "Then Jesus said to him, 'Put your sword back into its place. For all who take the sword will perish by the sword.'" The "put your sword back" is the Christological refusal of violent self-defense. The maxim — "all who take the sword will perish by the sword" — is the Christological principle of the kingdom's non-violent character. The same one who said "do not resist the one who is evil" (5:39) refuses to resist his own arrest. The kingdom advances not through violence but through the cross.
26.53: A direct revelation: "Do you think that I cannot appeal to my Father, and he will at once send me more than twelve legions of angels?" The rhetorical question — twelve legions of angels at his disposal — is the Christological demonstration of the voluntary nature of the passion. He could stop the arrest; he chooses not to. The twelve legions (72,000 angels) available on request is the measure of the submission: the one who has unlimited divine protection available chooses to drink the cup instead of calling for rescue.
26.54: A direct revelation: "But how then should the Scriptures be fulfilled, that it must be so?" The "how then should the Scriptures be fulfilled?" is the Christological hermeneutic of the passion: the cross is not an accident but the fulfillment of what the Scriptures required. The Son who knows the Scriptures better than anyone submits to the arrest because the Scriptures have spoken. The fulfillment of prophecy is not fate imposed on a passive victim but the active obedience of the Son who knows what the Father required.
26.55: A direct revelation: "At that hour Jesus said to the crowds, 'Have you come out as against a robber, with swords and clubs to capture me? Day after day I sat in the temple teaching, and you did not seize me.'" The Christological exposure of the arrest's absurdity: they treated the temple teacher as if he were a violent criminal, requiring an armed force in the night. The contrast — daily public teaching versus covert nighttime arrest — is the Christological indictment of the authorities' bad faith. They knew where he was; they chose the cover of darkness.
26.56: A direct fulfillment: "But all this has taken place that the Scriptures of the prophets might be fulfilled.' Then all the disciples left him and fled." The "all this" fulfilled in the Scriptures is the Christological reading of the passion: every element of the arrest — the betrayal, the sword-drawing, the fleeing disciples — is within the prophetic script. And then: "all the disciples left him and fled." The scattering of the sheep (v.31: Zech 13:7) is fulfilled. The Son faces the passion alone, as the Scriptures required and as the Father appointed.
26.57: A revelation of God: "Then those who had seized Jesus led him to Caiaphas the high priest, where the scribes and the elders had gathered." The Sanhedrin gathered at the high priest's house — in the middle of the night during Passover — is the Christological irregularity of the trial: the urgency of their timing violated their own legal procedures. The gathering of the scribes and elders is the institutional response to the one who has overturned their tables and condemned them with woes. The trial is predetermined; the arrest simply begins the final execution of the plan.
26.58: A revelation of God: "And Peter was following him at a distance, as far as the courtyard of the high priest, and going inside he sat with the guards to see the end." Peter following "at a distance" is the Christological description of compromised discipleship. He has not fled like the others (v.56) but he is not inside with Jesus either. "To see the end" — he does not yet know what the end will be. The distance between Peter and Jesus in the high priest's courtyard is the geography of the coming denial.
26.59: A revelation of God: "Now the chief priests and the whole council were seeking false testimony against Jesus that they might put him to death." The seeking of false testimony by the Sanhedrin is the Christological indictment of the entire trial: they begin with the verdict and seek evidence to support it. The established legal principle (Deut 17:6; 19:15: two or three witnesses required) is being subverted. The Son is condemned not by legal process but by the determination of those who have already decided to kill him.
26.60: A revelation of God: "But they found none, though many false witnesses came forward. At last two came forward." The inability to find false testimony — "they found none" — is the Christological irony of the perfect life being subjected to false accusation. The one who has lived perfectly according to Torah cannot be falsely accused consistently; the witnesses contradict each other (Mark 14:56: "their testimony did not agree"). At last, two witnesses are found for the one charge that will serve.
26.61: A revelation of God: "And said, 'This man said, 'I am able to destroy the temple of God, and to rebuild it in three days.''" The misquotation of John 2:19 ("destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up") is the Christological garbling of the resurrection prediction. Jesus said "you destroy"; they report "I am able to destroy." The three-day rebuilding — which John interprets as the resurrection of his body — is the charge that will be echoed at the cross (27:40: "you who would destroy the temple"). The false witness accidentally identifies the true sign Jesus offered.
26.62: A direct revelation: "And the high priest stood up and said, 'Have you no answer to make? What is it that these men testify against you?'" Caiaphas's question in the face of Jesus' silence is the Christological fulfillment of Isa 53:7: "he was oppressed, and he was afflicted, yet he opened not his mouth; like a lamb that is led to the slaughter, and like a sheep that before its shearers is silent." The suffering servant is present in the high priest's court. The silence before accusation is the silence of the innocent lamb.
26.63: A direct revelation: "But Jesus remained silent. And the high priest said to him, 'I adjure you by the living God, tell us if you are the Christ, the Son of God.'" The high priest's oath-adjuration — "by the living God" — is the Christological moment of forced disclosure: the Sanhedrin invokes the divine name to compel Jesus to answer. The question "are you the Christ, the Son of God?" is the question that has been developing throughout the entire Gospel. Under oath before the living God, Jesus will answer.
26.64: A direct revelation: "Jesus said to him, 'You have said so. But I tell you, from now on you will see the Son of Man seated at the right hand of Power and coming on the clouds of heaven.'" The climactic Christological confession: "you have said so" affirms the identification, and the addition of Dan 7:13 (Son of Man on the clouds) + Ps 110:1 (seated at the right hand of Power) is the dual citation that claims both the divine judge's eschatological role and the enthronement at the Father's right hand. The one being condemned declares his own future exaltation. The trial's verdict cannot stop what he announces.
26.65: A revelation of God: "Then the high priest tore his robes and said, 'He has uttered blasphemy. What further witnesses do we need? You have now heard his blasphemy.'" The tearing of robes (the high priest's sign of distress at heard blasphemy) is the Christological recognition that the claim has been heard and found unacceptable. The "blasphemy" verdict is the verdict on the Son of God's own self-identification. The one who could only be condemned for claiming what he actually is has finally spoken directly, and the Sanhedrin condemns it as blasphemy.
26.66: A revelation of God: "What is your judgment?' They answered, 'He deserves death.'" The Sanhedrin's death verdict on the blasphemy charge is the Christological judicial irony: they are right that blasphemy deserves death, but the charge of blasphemy is false — Jesus is exactly who he claimed to be. The verdict that condemns him is the verdict that the resurrection will overturn. The judges who pronounce death on the Son of God will see the Son of Man "coming on the clouds of heaven" (v.64).
26.67: A revelation of God: "Then they spit in his face and struck him. And some slapped him." The spitting and striking — the violation of the condemned man before execution — is the Christological fulfillment of Isa 50:6: "I gave my back to those who strike, and my cheeks to those who pull out the beard; I hid not my face from disgrace and spitting." The Servant who does not flinch from the spittle and blows is the one before whom the mockers will one day fall. The present humiliation is the Christological valley before the eschatological mountain.
26.68: A revelation of God: "Saying, 'Prophesy to us, you Christ! Who is it that struck you?'" The mocking demand to "prophesy" is the Christological irony at its deepest: the one who has just predicted the Son of Man's coming on the clouds of heaven, who predicted the disciples' flight and Peter's denial, who predicted Jerusalem's destruction and the end of the age — is asked to perform a parlor trick of identifying his attacker. The blindfolded (Luke 22:64) prophet is the one who sees all. The mockers do not know what they are mocking.
26.69: A revelation of God: "Now Peter was sitting outside in the courtyard. And a servant girl came up to him and said, 'You also were with Jesus the Galilean.'" The servant girl's identification of Peter — "you also were with Jesus the Galilean" — is the Christological test of public allegiance. The one who vowed to die with Jesus (v.35) is being asked by a servant girl to acknowledge the connection. The "also" suggests she has identified others; Peter is in a chain of identification that he is about to break.
26.70: A revelation of God: "But he denied it before them all, saying, 'I do not know what you mean.'" The first denial — "I do not know what you mean" — is the evasion before the explicit lie. Peter does not say "I do not know him" yet; he claims incomprehension. The Christological bearing: the denial moves from evasion to explicit lie to sworn denial — a progression that mirrors the way compromise escalates under pressure.
26.71: A revelation of God: "And when he went out to the entrance, another servant girl saw him and said to the bystanders, 'This man was with Jesus of Nazareth.'" The second identification — at the entrance, by another servant girl to bystanders — forces Peter into the public square of denial. The "with Jesus of Nazareth" is the specific association: not just a follower of a teacher but a member of the specific Galilean's entourage that has disrupted Jerusalem all week.
26.72: A revelation of God: "And again he denied it with an oath: 'I do not know the man.'" The second denial — "with an oath" — escalates the Christological rejection: Peter invokes divine witness to his claim that he does not know Jesus. The same Peter who confessed "you are the Christ, the Son of the living God" (16:16) now swears an oath that he does not know the man. The descent from confession to oath-denial is the measure of untested faith under pressure.
26.73: A revelation of God: "After a little while the bystanders came up and said to Peter, 'Certainly you too are one of them, for your accent betrays you.'" The Galilean accent that betrays Peter is the Christological evidence that ties him to the Galilean teacher. The speech that identifies him as one of Jesus' company is the ironic fulfillment: Peter's voice, which has confessed Jesus as the Christ, now identifies him as a follower even as his words deny it. The body witnesses what the mouth denies.
26.74: A revelation of God: "Then he began to invoke a curse on himself and to swear, 'I do not know the man.' And immediately the rooster crowed." The third denial — with self-cursing and swearing — is the fullest betrayal of the Christological confession. The invocation of curse is the opposite of the beatitudes. And then: the rooster crow. The Christological precision of the prediction fulfilled — exactly as Jesus said, at exactly the moment Jesus said. The denial did not catch Jesus by surprise; he named it before it happened.
26.75: A revelation of God: "And Peter remembered the saying of Jesus, 'Before the rooster crows, you will deny me three times.' And he went out and wept bitterly." The remembering of Jesus' word is the Christological moment of conviction: the word Jesus spoke at the table is fulfilled, and Peter knows it. The bitter weeping is the Christological response to having denied the one he loves — not the despair of Judas (27:3-5: he hanged himself) but the grief that will become the foundation for the restoration that the Risen Lord will offer (John 21:15-19). Peter's tears are the beginning of the road back.

27.46: A direct revelation: my God, my God, why have you forsaken me? The cry of dereliction is the most difficult Christological moment in the Gospels — the eternal Son experiencing genuine God-forsakenness as the penalty of sin he is bearing for others. This is not merely the quoting of a Psalm for devotional comfort (though the Psalm ends in vindication); it is the actual experience of what Paul calls being made sin for us (2 Cor 5:21) and being cursed for us (Gal 3:13). The darkness, the abandoned cry, and the torn curtain together form the Christological declaration: God was in Christ, in the place of the God-forsaken, reconciling the world to himself.
27.51: A fulfillment: the curtain of the temple was torn in two from top to bottom. The rending of the veil that separated the holy of holies from the holy place is the Christological climax of Matthew's temple-theology: the barrier between God and humanity that the entire Levitical system presupposed and maintained is destroyed by Christ's death. Access to the divine presence, which required the high priest alone once a year with blood, is now open to all — the way into the most holy place has been opened permanently through Christ's body (Heb 10:20). Matthew adds the opened tombs and the resurrection of the saints (vv.52-53) as the first-fruits of the general resurrection inaugurated by Christ's death.
27.1: A revelation of God: "When morning came, all the chief priests and the elders of the people took counsel against Jesus to put him to death." The early morning council — the formal Sanhedrin vote after the nighttime interrogation — is the Christological fulfillment of the passion predictions: Jesus is being handed over by the religious leadership. The "morning" is the deliberate timing of the legal formality; the nighttime session was the real decision. The Christological irony: the most religious people in Jerusalem are meeting at dawn to kill the Son of God.
27.2: A revelation of God: "And they bound him and led him away and delivered him over to Pilate the governor." The binding and delivery to Pilate is the fulfillment of 20:19 ("they will deliver him over to the Gentiles"). The passive "delivered over" (paredōkan) is the passion verb that runs through the entire narrative — from Judas delivering him to the chief priests to the chief priests delivering him to Pilate. The divine passive underlies each human delivery: the Son is being given over according to the Father's plan.
27.3: A direct fulfillment: "Then when Judas, his betrayer, saw that Jesus was condemned, he changed his mind and brought back the thirty pieces of silver to the chief priests and the elders." The change of mind (metamelomai, regret rather than metanoiō, repentance) is the Christological distinction: Judas feels regret but does not turn to the one he betrayed. The return of the silver is the Christological reversal of the transaction — but the reversal cannot undo the betrayal. The money returned does not buy back what was sold.
27.4: A direct fulfillment: "Saying, 'I have sinned by betraying innocent blood.' They said, 'What is that to us? See to it yourself.'" Judas' confession — "innocent blood" (haima athōon) — is the Christological testimony of the betrayer himself. Even the one who sold him knows him to be innocent. The chief priests' dismissal ("see to it yourself") is the Christological abandonment: the institution that paid for the betrayal refuses to receive the confession. The betrayer is left alone with his guilt.
27.5: A shadow: "And throwing down the pieces of silver into the temple, he departed, and he went and hanged himself." The suicide of Judas is the Christological contrast to Peter's response to his own failure: Peter wept bitterly and was restored; Judas despaired and died. The throwing of the silver into the temple is the final act of a man who cannot live with what he has done. The Christological bearing: the thirty pieces of silver thrown in the temple will fulfill Scripture (v.9-10) even through the despair of the one who threw them.
27.6: A direct fulfillment: "But the chief priests, taking the pieces of silver, said, 'It is not lawful to put them into the treasury, since it is blood money.'" The irony of scrupulousness about blood money — by those who just used it to purchase an innocent man's death — is the Christological exposure of casuistry: they will not defile the treasury but have already defiled the Messiah. The same pattern as the oath-disputes of ch.23: meticulous about small rules while committing the largest violation.
27.7: A direct fulfillment: "So they took counsel and bought with them the potter's field as a burial place for strangers." The purchase of the potter's field with the blood money is the Christological fulfillment in motion: the exact use of the returned money fulfills what Zechariah prophesied. The "potter's field" becomes a permanent monument to the transaction — the field purchased with the price of the Son is still called "Field of Blood" (v.8).
27.8: A direct fulfillment: "Therefore that field has been called the Field of Blood to this day." The naming of the field — permanent, public, remembered "to this day" — is the Christological monument to the betrayal. The land itself bears the testimony: this is the field that was purchased with the price of innocent blood. The topography of Jerusalem carries the evidence of what was done to the Son.
27.9: A direct fulfillment: "Then was fulfilled what had been spoken by the prophet Jeremiah, saying, 'And they took the thirty pieces of silver, the price of him on whom a price had been set by some of the sons of Israel.'" The fulfillment attribution to Jeremiah (with quotation from Zech 11:12-13, possibly combined with Jer 19:1-13) is the Christological reading of the prophetic literature: the Son's betrayal price was named centuries before. The thirty pieces of silver as the slave price (Exod 21:32) and the price of the rejected shepherd (Zech 11) are both fulfilled in the one transaction.
27.10: A direct fulfillment: "And they gave them for the potter's field, as the Lord directed me." The "as the Lord directed me" closes the prophetic citation with the divine agency behind the entire transaction. The Christological fulfillment: even the decision of the chief priests to purchase the potter's field with the blood money was "directed" by the Lord — the divine economy works through and despite the choices of human actors. The betrayal and its monetary consequence are within the Father's purpose.
27.11: A direct revelation: "Now Jesus stood before the governor, and the governor asked him, 'Are you the King of the Jews?' Jesus said, 'You have said so.'" The "you have said so" (sy legeis) before Pilate is the same formula as before Caiaphas (26:64) and Judas (26:25). The affirmation is genuine but turns the question back on the questioner: Pilate has identified the truth. The "King of the Jews" is a political title; the Christological reality exceeds it — he is the King of kings, whose throne is not in Jerusalem's political structures but in the eschatological kingdom.
27.12: A direct fulfillment: "But when he was accused by the chief priests and elders, he gave no answer." The silence of Jesus before accusation is the Christological fulfillment of Isa 53:7: "he opened not his mouth; like a lamb that is led to the slaughter." The one who spoke with authority in the temple, who silenced every questioner, who pronounced the seven woes — now stands in silence before the accusations of those he silenced. The silence is not defeat but the chosen posture of the one who submits to the Father's will.
27.13: A revelation of God: "Then Pilate said to him, 'Do you not hear how many things they testify against you?'" Pilate's astonishment at the silence — "do you not hear?" — is the Christological marvel of the governor who has never encountered someone who does not defend himself. The accusations are many; the defense is nothing. The Christological inversion: the one who has the most to say and the most grounds to say it chooses silence. The lamb before its shearers is silent.
27.14: A direct fulfillment: "But he gave him no answer, not even to a single charge, so that the governor was greatly amazed." Pilate's amazement at the total silence is the Christological effect of the suffering servant's posture. The governor who has seen every kind of accused person has never seen this: the innocent who does not protest. The amazement is the beginning of Pilate's private conviction of Jesus' innocence — which will be confirmed by his wife's dream (v.19) and his own handwashing (v.24).
27.15: A revelation of God: "Now at the feast the governor was accustomed to release for the crowd any one prisoner whom they wanted." The Passover custom of prisoner release is the Christological setting for the Barabbas exchange. The annual release of one prisoner at Passover frames the ultimate Passover exchange: the guilty is released, the innocent is condemned. The one who is the true Passover lamb (1 Cor 5:7) is rejected for the release of the criminal.
27.16: A revelation of God: "And they had then a notorious prisoner called Barabbas." Barabbas (bar-Abba, "son of the father") is the Christological contrast: the "son of the father" who is a murderer (Mark 15:7) is released while the true Son of the Father is condemned. The name itself is an ironic commentary. Barabbas represents every sinner released at the cost of the innocent — the substitutionary logic of the atonement enacted in the Passover drama of the courtyard.
27.17: A revelation of God: "So when they had gathered, Pilate said to them, 'Whom do you want me to release for you: Barabbas, or Jesus who is called Christ?'" Pilate's question — Barabbas or "Jesus who is called Christ" — is the Christological choice offered to every generation: the criminal or the Messiah. The crowd's choice of Barabbas is the choice of humanity for the guilty over the innocent, the known criminal over the unknown king. The one who is called Christ is the title that will be vindicated at the resurrection.
27.18: A revelation of God: "For he knew that it was out of envy that they had delivered him up." Pilate's perception — "out of envy" (dia phthonon) — is the Christological insight of an outsider who reads the situation more clearly than the insiders. The envy of the religious leaders toward Jesus (whose crowds, authority, and teaching they cannot match) is the political motivation behind the theological charge of blasphemy. The Christological irony: the pagan governor sees what the high priest refuses to see.
27.19: A revelation of God: "Besides, while he was sitting on the judgment seat, his wife sent word to him, 'Have nothing to do with that righteous man, for I have suffered much because of him today in a dream.'" Pilate's wife's dream warning — "that righteous man" (tō dikaiō ekeinō) — is the second Gentile testimony to Jesus' innocence (after Pilate's own perception in v.18). The dream is the divine warning that Pilate will ignore. The Christological pattern: the Gentiles who have no prior relationship with Israel's Messiah recognize him more clearly than those who planned his death.
27.20: A revelation of God: "Now the chief priests and the elders persuaded the crowd to ask for Barabbas and destroy Jesus." The manipulation of the crowd by the chief priests is the Christological political machinery of the passion: the same crowd that cried "Hosanna to the Son of David" (21:9) will now cry "crucify him" (v.22-23) because the chief priests have worked on them. The crowd is not monolithic; it is the target of deliberate manipulation by those with power.
27.21: A revelation of God: "The governor again said to them, 'Which of the two do you want me to release for you?' And they said, 'Barabbas.'" The crowd's choice of Barabbas is the Christological rejection enacted publicly. The one whom the crowd asked about in 21:10 ("who is this?") and whom some identified as the prophet (21:11) is now exchanged for a murderer. The Passover release that should set the captive free becomes the release of the guilty and the condemnation of the innocent.
27.22: A revelation of God: "Pilate said to them, 'Then what shall I do with Jesus who is called Christ?' They all said, 'Let him be crucified!'" The question "what shall I do with Jesus who is called Christ?" is the Christological question of history — the question every person and community must answer. The crowd's "crucify him!" is the specific answer of the passion, but the question is universal. The one who is "called Christ" is the one whose fate every generation determines by its response to him.
