"Blessed by my Father" connects the Son's judgment to the Father's blessing — the shared verdict of the divine persons. The kingdom prepared "from the foundation of the world" is the eschatological gift that was always the Son's to give to those the Father has given him (John 17:2).
25.35: A direct revelation: "For I was hungry and you gave me food, I was thirsty and you gave me drink, I was a stranger and you welcomed me." The first three identifications — hungry, thirsty, stranger — are the vulnerable conditions in which the King identifies himself with those who suffer. The Christological claim is extraordinary: the one who fed five thousand was himself hungry; the one who offered living water was thirsty; the Son who was "from Nazareth of Galilee" (21:11) knows what it is to be a stranger. The King speaks from genuine solidarity with the conditions he names.
25.36: A direct revelation: "I was naked and you clothed me, I was sick and you visited me, I was in prison and you came to me." The three further identifications — naked, sick, imprisoned — continue the catalog of vulnerability. The one who was stripped of his garments at the crucifixion (27:35) knows nakedness; the one who healed the sick knows sickness from the inside of compassion; the one who was imprisoned by the Sanhedrin before being handed to Pilate knows incarceration. The King's identification with the vulnerable is not metaphorical but embodied in his own passion.
25.37: A revelation of God: "Then the righteous will answer him, saying, 'Lord, when did we see you hungry and feed you, or thirsty and give you drink?'" The righteous are surprised — they did not know they were serving the King. The Christological unselfconsciousness of genuine service: the sheep who cared for the vulnerable did not calculate the eschatological significance of their actions. They served because the need was before them, not because they expected eschatological commendation. The surprise is the mark of the genuinely righteous action: done without awareness of its ultimate significance.
25.38: A revelation of God: "And when did we see you a stranger and welcome you, or naked and clothe you?" The continuation of the righteous's surprised question reinforces the Christological anonymity of genuine service: they saw no distinguishing mark on the hungry, the stranger, the naked. They saw need and met it. The Christological revelation that the King was present in each needy person is the eschatological disclosure of what was always true but invisible during the age of service.
25.39: A revelation of God: "And when did we see you sick or in prison and visit you?" The final pair of the surprise-question completes the catalog. The Christological point: the sheep who visited the sick and imprisoned were not doing so as a strategy for eschatological reward — they did not know who they were serving. Their service to the anonymous needy was service to the anonymous King. The disclosure at the judgment is the unveiling of the identity that was always present but hidden.
25.40: A direct revelation: "And the King will answer them, 'Truly, I say to you, as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me.'" The "you did it to me" is the highest Christological identification in the entire Gospel. The King identifies himself with the most vulnerable — "the least of these my brothers." The phrase "my brothers" identifies the vulnerable as the King's family — those with whom he is in solidarity. The Christological solidarity: what is done to the least is done to the one who called the least his brothers. The identity is real, not merely symbolic.
25.41: A direct revelation: "Then he will say to those on his left, 'Depart from me, you cursed, into the eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels.'" The "depart from me" — the words of eschatological exclusion — echo 7:23 ("I never knew you; depart from me"). The eternal fire prepared for the devil and his angels is the Christological specificity of the judgment's destination: the punishment was not prepared for humans but for the adversary and his agents. Those who receive it have aligned themselves, by their failure of service, with the adversary's refusal of the vulnerable.
25.42: A direct revelation: "For I was hungry and you gave me no food, I was thirsty and you gave me no drink." The same six categories of need — now unmet — are the Christological indictment of the goats. The same King who identified himself with the vulnerable now identifies the failure to serve the vulnerable as failure to serve him. The symmetry is precise: every act of service and every failure to serve is weighed against the same standard — the King who was hungry, thirsty, naked, sick, imprisoned.
25.43: A direct revelation: "I was a stranger and you did not welcome me, naked and you did not clothe me, sick and in prison and you did not visit me." The three further failures complete the indictment. The Christological bearing: the neglect of the vulnerable is not a passive failure but an active refusal — the need was present and was passed by. The one who identifies himself with the needy knows what it is to be passed by, and the memory of each passing becomes the content of the judgment.
25.44: A revelation of God: "Then they also will answer, saying, 'Lord, when did we see you hungry or thirsty or a stranger or naked or sick or in prison, and did not minister to you?'" The goats' surprised question mirrors the sheep's: they did not know either. The Christological symmetry of the ignorance is instructive: both groups were unaware of the King's presence in the needy. The difference is not awareness but action: the sheep acted when they saw need; the goats did not. The question "when did we see you?" reveals the category error — they would have acted if they had recognized the King, but that is precisely the point they missed.
25.45: A direct revelation: "Then he will answer them, saying, 'Truly, I say to you, as you did not do it to one of the least of these, you did not do it to me.'" The negative form of the great identification — "you did not do it to me" — closes the Christological circle. Every failure to serve the needy is a failure to serve the King. The "least of these" who were passed by were the King in disguise; every act of passing by was an act of rejecting the sovereign. The judgment reveals that the goats were making choices of ultimate consequence while thinking they were making choices of no consequence.
25.46: A direct revelation: "And these will go away into eternal punishment, but the righteous into eternal life." The final verse of ch.25 and the final verse of the fifth discourse presents the two eternal destinations: eternal punishment and eternal life. The Christological framing: both destinies are determined by the same judge, the Son of Man on his throne, who is simultaneously the King who identifies with the vulnerable and the judge who separates on the basis of that identification. The discourse that began with ten virgins waiting for the bridegroom ends with all nations sorted before the King — the same person, the same standard, the same eschatological reality.
